key_insight: α-synuclein seed amplification assay (SAA) shows strong real-world ability to detect α-synuclein pathology in memory clinic patients, while standard Alzheimer fluid biomarkers add little for identifying co-pathology.
clinical_relevance: Routine SAA testing could improve diagnostic stratification, especially for Lewy body dementia and Alzheimer disease patients with hidden α-synuclein co-pathology.
method: 398 patients from the multicenter ALZAN memory clinic cohort
CSF and plasma Alzheimer biomarkers measured alongside α-synuclein RT-QuIC SAA
Cognitive status assessed with MMSE and diagnoses confirmed by two senior neurologists
evidence_signal: In the cohort, SAA was positive in 95.0% of Lewy body dementia cases and 15.8% of Alzheimer disease cases, with reported sensitivity of 95.0% and specificity of 93.1% for distinguishing LBD from non-LBD/non-AD patients.
watch_item: Findings come from a memory clinic cohort and do not prove that AD CSF or blood biomarkers are useful for detecting α-synuclein co-pathology in broader or earlier-stage populations.
paper_title: Routine implementation of α-synuclein Seed Amplification Assays reveals high diagnostic performance and the limited value of Alzheimer disease fluid biomarkers for detecting α-synuclein co-pathology
